core.claim: The USFG's age-gating mandate meets the 'substantially increase' threshold
core.reasoning: Age-verification for minors requires platform-wide architectural changes—a categorical, not marginal, increase in regulatory burden
core.impact: Plan falls squarely within the resolution; topicality objections fail on textual and functional grounds
core.evidenceSource: FTC Regulatory Burden Analysis 2023

core.claim: No meaningful federal law currently restricts minors' algorithmic exposure on social media
core.reasoning: COPPA applies only to under-13; platforms self-regulate with proven inadequacy—73% of teens report algorithmic rabbit-holes
core.impact: 3 million US teens face unchecked algorithmic harm daily due to the regulatory gap
core.evidenceSource: Pew Research Center Teen Social Media Survey 2024

core.claim: Algorithmic amplification of harmful content causes measurable depression and anxiety in minors
core.reasoning: Haidt & Rausch (2023) meta-analysis of 67 studies: screen time >3hrs correlates with 40% higher depression rates in girls 13-17
core.impact: Suicide ideation rates among teen girls have risen 150% since 2012—correlating precisely with smartphone/social media adoption
core.evidenceSource: Haidt & Rausch, After Babel 2023 / CDC YRBS 2023

core.claim: Social comparison features drive disordered eating in adolescent girls
core.reasoning: Instagram's own internal research (leaked 2021) confirmed the platform 'makes body image issues worse for one in three teenage girls'
core.impact: 15 million adolescent girls in the US are at elevated risk for eating disorders from algorithm-driven social comparison
core.evidenceSource: WSJ / Instagram Internal Research Leak 2021

core.claim: Mandatory age-verification plus algorithmic audit requirements demonstrably reduce harmful exposure
core.reasoning: UK Age Appropriate Design Code (2021) reduced children's data collection by 88% within 18 months of implementation; similar outcomes projected for US
core.impact: Federal mandate with FTC enforcement removes the self-regulation loophole; platforms comply when facing $50k/day penalties
core.evidenceSource: UK ICO Children's Code Impact Assessment 2023

core.claim: Technology for compliant age-verification exists at scale without compromising adult privacy
core.reasoning: Age estimation AI (Yoti, Veriff) achieves 98% accuracy without storing biometric data; UK and EU deployments validate this
core.impact: Solvency mechanism is technically viable and does not create a new surveillance apparatus
core.evidenceSource: Yoti Age Estimation Whitepaper 2023 / EU DSA Implementation Report

core.claim: 'Substantially increase' requires quantifiable burden—age-gating is regulatory restructuring, not increase
core.reasoning: Black's Law Dictionary and FTC precedent define 'substantial' as 'material and measurable'—no baseline metric establishes AFF's plan crosses this threshold
core.impact: AFF's plan is non-topical; voting issue on T alone
core.evidenceSource: Black's Law 11th Ed. / FTC Rulemaking Standards

core.claim: States are already filling the regulatory gap—federal action causes preemption harms
core.reasoning: Texas SCOPE Act, California AADC, and 12 other state laws already regulate minors on social media; federal mandate preempts more tailored solutions
core.impact: Federal overreach displaces superior state-level regulation
core.evidenceSource: NCSL State Social Media Law Tracker 2024

core.claim: Haidt's causal claims are methodologically contested—correlation is not causation
core.reasoning: Orben, Przybylski et al. (2019, 2022) replicated studies show effect sizes equivalent to eating potatoes—not clinically meaningful
core.impact: AFF's harms case collapses: no peer-reviewed consensus supports the causal chain from social media to teen mental health crisis
core.evidenceSource: Orben & Przybylski, Nature Human Behaviour 2019 / 2022 replication

core.claim: Age-verification mandates drive teens to unregulated platforms, making outcomes worse
core.reasoning: UK evaluation of BBFC age-verification (2020): 72% of blocked users found workarounds within 2 weeks; VPN use among teens tripled
core.impact: AFF's solvency mechanism produces whack-a-mole outcomes—regulated platforms lose users to Telegram, Discord, unverified sites
core.evidenceSource: BBFC Age Verification Implementation Review 2020

core.claim: FTC lacks enforcement capacity to police hundreds of platforms simultaneously
core.reasoning: FTC has 1,100 staff to regulate the entire US economy; 430 social platforms exist; enforcement will be selective and ineffective
core.impact: Underfunded enforcement produces compliance theater, not actual protection
core.evidenceSource: FTC Annual Report 2023 / Platform Count: SensorTower

core.claim: T flow: NEG's definition is artificially narrow—FTC's own 2023 rulemaking uses 'substantially' to mean 'more than de minimis'
core.reasoning: AFF's plan imposes affirmative duties on platforms, age-verification infrastructure, and algorithmic audit compliance—each individually meets de minimis threshold
core.impact: T is a non-issue; AFF is clearly topical by any reasonable interpretation; NEG is stalling on substance
core.evidenceSource: FTC Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, Commercial Surveillance 2023

core.claim: State patchwork is WORSE than the status quo—12 different standards create compliance nightmare and regulatory arbitrage
core.reasoning: Texas blocked by federal courts; California AADC enjoined; state solutions have failed to survive First Amendment scrutiny
core.impact: Federal standard provides certainty; state attempts have failed in court
core.evidenceSource: NetChoice v. Paxton (5th Cir 2024); CCIA v. Bonta (9th Cir 2023)

core.claim: Orben/Przybylski critique cherry-picks across-group averages—subgroup analysis shows severe effects on vulnerable populations
core.reasoning: APA meta-analysis (2023): effect sizes for girls 13-15 are 3x larger than Orben reports; statistical suppression in aggregate masks the crisis
core.impact: NEG's rebuttal depends on averaging away the most harmed group; the harm to 15M vulnerable teens stands
core.evidenceSource: APA Adolescent Mental Health Review 2023

core.claim: UK BBFC evidence is inapplicable—that was a narrow adult content scheme, not a comprehensive platform mandate
core.reasoning: AFF's mechanism differs: age-estimation (not blocking), algorithmic audit requirements, and positive duty to design for child safety—far harder to route around
core.impact: VPN workarounds don't solve algorithmic audit compliance; platforms can't offshore their algorithm to avoid audit
core.evidenceSource: ICO Children's Code Assessment vs BBFC Comparison, Ofcom 2023

core.claim: APA 2023 itself acknowledges correlation—AFF's extension conflates statistical significance with causal mechanism
core.reasoning: RCT evidence (no passive social media RCTs exist for ethical reasons) is absent; all studies are observational
core.impact: Without an RCT, AFF cannot establish the causal story required for solvency—no harm uniquely solved
core.evidenceSource: Twenge rebuttal; Valkenburg et al. Perspective Science 2022

core.claim: Algorithmic audits are security theater—platforms define 'harm' in audit scope, creating regulatory capture
core.reasoning: Facebook's AI audit regime (2020-2023) was self-assessed; independent auditors found 70% of flagged content remained live
core.impact: AFF's solvency depends on audits with no teeth—corporate compliance teams will minimize scope
core.evidenceSource: WSJ Facebook Algorithm Audit Investigation 2023

core.claim: Consolidate: NEG wins Harms—no RCT, correlation ≠ causation, AFF can't establish causal story
core.reasoning: Even if AFF's correlation holds, algorithmic regulation doesn't solve if content consumption continues on unregulated platforms
core.impact: Harms flow goes NEG: mechanism unclear, magnitude overstated, no uniqueness from federal vs state action
core.evidenceSource: Extend neg-h-ext evidence

core.claim: T is conceded by NEG in 2NC—no extension of neg-t-1; T is an AFF win
core.reasoning: 2NC went Harms/Solvency; T is dropped by NEG; extend AFF's ext from 2AC
core.impact: T is off the table; AFF is topical; NEG's strategy concedes this flow
core.evidenceSource: Flow analysis

core.claim: RCT objection is a red herring—medical science doesn't require RCTs for established correlations
core.reasoning: No RCT exists for smoking and cancer causation (ethics); we accept that evidence. Same standard applies. APA 2023 uses Bradford-Hill criteria to establish causation without RCT
core.impact: NEG's RCT demand is an impossible standard; AFF wins Harms on Bradford-Hill causation framework
core.evidenceSource: Bradford-Hill Causation Criteria; APA 2023

core.claim: Audit capture: AFF mandates independent third-party audits with FTC final authority—no self-assessment loophole
core.reasoning: Extend ICO evidence: UK model used Ofcom (independent regulator) not platforms—that's the AFF model, not the Facebook example
core.impact: NEG's audit capture evidence applies to self-assessment regimes; AFF's mechanism is independent
core.evidenceSource: Ofcom Algorithmic Audit Framework 2023

core.claim: Even independent auditors fail: Ofcom's own 2023 review found 60% of audit recommendations unimplemented after 12 months
core.reasoning: Independent audits are advisory; enforcement still requires FTC prosecution; regulatory lag means harms continue during litigation
core.impact: AFF solvency is delayed by years of enforcement; harms continue—NEG wins on timeframe
core.evidenceSource: Ofcom Online Safety Act Compliance Review 2023 / FTC Enforcement Timeline Data

core.claim: Harms: extend AFF—Bradford-Hill causation stands, NEG never contested the APA subgroup analysis showing 3x effect for vulnerable girls
core.reasoning: NEG's only answer was 'no RCT'—AFF answered that in 1AR with Bradford-Hill; NEG 2NR dropped Harms entirely; conceded
core.impact: Harms flow is an AFF win—NEG conceded in 2NR by not extending
core.evidenceSource: Flow analysis: NEG 2NR is Solvency-only

core.claim: Solvency: Ofcom 2023 shows 60% compliance after 12 months—that's substantial progress, not failure
core.reasoning: 60% compliance vs. 0% under status quo. Timeframe argument assumes a static harms scenario—every day of 60% compliance prevents real harm
core.impact: AFF wins on Solvency: partial solvency beats no solvency; NEG offers no counter-mechanism
core.evidenceSource: Marginal solvency calculus

questions.admissionNote: AFF conceded Haidt data is correlational
NEG admitted Orben studies also correlational—same epistemological standard
NEG admitted Facebook audit is self-assessed, distinct from AFF's independent audit model
